solution: 
```python
class Solution:
    def countOfAtoms(self, formula: str) -> str:
        stack = [{}]
        i = 0
        while i < len(formula):
            if formula[i] == '(':
                stack.append({})
                i += 1
            elif formula[i] == ')':
                i += 1
                j = i
                while j < len(formula) and formula[j].isdigit():
                    j += 1
                count = int(formula[i:j] or '1')
                i = j
                top = stack.pop()
                for key, val in top.items():
                    stack[-1][key] = stack[-1].get(key, 0) + val * count
            else:
                j = i + 1
                while j < len(formula) and formula[j].islower():
                    j += 1
                atom = formula[i:j]
                i = j
                j = i
                while j < len(formula) and formula[j].isdigit():
                    j += 1
                count = int(formula[i:j] or '1')
                i = j
                stack[-1][atom] = stack[-1].get(atom, 0) + count
        return ''.join(f'{k}{v}' if v > 1 else k for k, v in sorted(stack[0].items()))
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(N)
space complexity: O(N)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution uses a stack to parse the chemical formula. When encountering an '(', it pushes a new dictionary onto the stack. When encountering a ')', it pops the top dictionary, multiplies its counts by the following number (if any), and adds them to the previous dictionary. Finally, it constructs the output string by sorting the items in the last dictionary and appending each key and value (if greater than 1).
